enrichment.short_description: Known is an open-source social publishing platform that lets individuals and groups post blogs, photos, notes and status updates to a self-owned site while syndicating copies out to social networks.
enrichment.seo_title: Known (withknown.com) Review: Open-Source Social Publishing Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what Known (withknown.com) offers: an open-source, self-hosted social publishing engine for blogs, photos and group sites, built on IndieWeb principles.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Known, published at withknown.com, is an open-source social publishing platform co-founded by Ben Werdmuller and Erin Jo Richey. It was incubated at the Matter Ventures media-startup accelerator in San Francisco and launched publicly on September 11, 2014. Known's core idea is to let a person or a group (a classroom, a research team, a community) run their own collaborative website where multiple authors post short or long-form content -- blog posts, status updates, photos, bookmarks, and other media types -- to one shared, self-owned site, then optionally syndicate copies of that content out to third-party social networks such as Twitter and Facebook. This POSSE-style approach (publish on your own site, syndicate elsewhere) places Known firmly within the IndieWeb movement, which Werdmuller has been involved with since his earlier project, the open-source community platform Elgg. Known is distributed as free, installable open-source software that a user or organization downloads and runs on their own PHP-capable web server, similar in distribution model to WordPress, rather than as a centrally hosted SaaS product with tiered subscription pricing. Documented deployments have included university and media communities such as Harvard, KQED, and Davidson College, with KQED's implementation recognized by a media-literacy award from NAMLE. Functionally, Known supports multiple content types per post (text, photo, checkin, event, and more), a bookmarklet for quick posting from other web pages, hashtag-based organization, custom domains, custom CSS and JavaScript, HTML and WYSIWYG editing, and multiple machine-readable feed formats (RSS, XML, JSON, KML) for interoperability with other tools. Privacy controls allow site owners to run fully public sites, private single-user blogs, or closed group/community discussion spaces. Because Known is self-hosted, there is no vendor lock-in and no recurring platform fee beyond the user's own hosting costs; the tradeoff is that installation, updates, and maintenance are the operator's responsibility rather than a managed service's. As an open-source project driven largely by a small core team and community rather than a large commercial vendor, Known has seen slower feature cadence in recent years compared to hosted competitors like WordPress.com or Ghost, but it remains available, documented, and installable for anyone who wants a lightweight, ownership-first alternative to centralized blogging and social platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Known
paragraphs: Known is an open-source social publishing engine built around the idea that individuals and groups should own their content rather than renting space on a closed social network. A single Known installation can serve as a personal blog or as a shared site for a whole community, with unlimited collaborators able to post to the same domain.

The project was co-founded by Ben Werdmuller and Erin Jo Richey and formally launched in 2014 after being incubated at the Matter Ventures media-startup program in San Francisco. Werdmuller's earlier work on the open-source platform Elgg fed directly into Known's design philosophy of decentralized, user-owned publishing.

Known follows the IndieWeb principle of publishing on your own site first and syndicating copies elsewhere (POSSE), rather than posting natively into a third-party platform's feed and losing control over that content.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: A single post in Known can be a blog article, a short status update, a photo, a bookmark, or another supported content type, all managed from one interface. A browser bookmarklet lets users quickly save or comment on pages they find elsewhere on the web.

Sites support custom domains, custom CSS and JavaScript, hashtag-based content organization, and both raw HTML and WYSIWYG editing. Output feeds are available in RSS, XML, JSON and KML so other tools and services can consume a site's content programmatically.
Because it is self-hosted, Known gives operators full control over privacy: a site can be fully public, a private single-author journal, or a closed group space for a defined set of collaborators.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Known is distributed as free, open-source software that a user downloads and installs on their own PHP hosting, so there is no vendor subscription fee for the software itself -- the only ongoing cost is whatever web hosting the operator chooses.

There is no current tiered commercial hosted plan comparable to WordPress.com; anyone who wants a managed Known site is generally expected to install it on standard shared or cloud PHP hosting themselves or work with a developer to do so.

features.title: Multi-format posting
features.description: Publish blog posts, short status updates, photos, bookmarks and other content types from a single composer.

features.title: Multi-author sites
features.description: Run a shared community or group site with an unlimited number of collaborators posting to one domain.

features.title: POSSE syndication
features.description: Publish content on your own Known site first, then automatically push copies out to connected social networks.

features.title: Bookmarklet
features.description: Save links and quickly post commentary on pages found anywhere else on the web.

features.title: Custom domains and styling
features.description: Attach your own domain and customize appearance with custom CSS and JavaScript.

features.title: Multiple feed formats
features.description: Expose site content as RSS, XML, JSON and KML feeds for interoperability with other tools.

features.title: Privacy controls
features.description: Run a fully public site, a private personal journal, or a closed group discussion space.

features.title: Self-hosted, open-source core
features.description: Install and run Known on your own PHP server for full data ownership with no platform lock-in.

pros: Free and open source with no recurring platform fee
Full data ownership since content lives on the operator's own domain and server
Supports both single-author blogs and multi-author community sites from one codebase
Built on open IndieWeb standards for content portability and syndication
cons: Requires the user to find and manage their own PHP hosting and updates
Slower development pace than commercial hosted platforms like WordPress.com or Ghost
Smaller plugin and theme ecosystem compared to mainstream CMS platforms
No official managed hosted-plan option comparable to competitors

faqs.question: Is Known free to use
faqs.answer: Yes, Known is free, open-source software. You install it on your own hosting, so the only cost is whatever web hosting you choose.

faqs.question: Who created Known
faqs.answer: Known was co-founded by Ben Werdmuller and Erin Jo Richey and launched in 2014 after being incubated at the Matter Ventures accelerator in San Francisco.

faqs.question: What is POSSE and how does Known use it
faqs.answer: POSSE stands for Publish on your Own Site, Syndicate Elsewhere. Known lets you publish content to your own domain first and then automatically syndicate copies to connected social networks.

faqs.question: Can multiple people post to the same Known site
faqs.answer: Yes, Known supports unlimited collaborators on a single site, making it suitable for community, classroom, or team publishing as well as personal blogging.

faqs.question: Does Known offer a hosted version
faqs.answer: Known is primarily distributed as self-hosted open-source software; there is no widely advertised managed hosting tier comparable to WordPress.com.

faqs.question: What content types can I publish with Known
faqs.answer: Known supports blog posts, short status updates, photos, bookmarks, and other content types from a single posting interface.

faqs.question: Is Known part of the IndieWeb movement
faqs.answer: Yes, Known follows IndieWeb principles around content ownership and portability, and its founder was previously involved in the open-source Elgg community platform.

use_cases.title: Personal ownership-first blogging
use_cases.description: Individuals who want to own their content and syndicate copies to social networks instead of posting natively into closed platforms.

use_cases.title: Community and classroom publishing
use_cases.description: Groups such as classrooms or research teams running a shared multi-author site under one domain.

use_cases.title: Media and journalism microsites
use_cases.description: Small newsroom or media projects wanting a lightweight, self-hosted alternative to larger CMS platforms.

enrichment.short_description: Wix is a cloud-based website builder and business platform that lets anyone design, host and manage websites, online stores and web apps using drag-and-drop tools or AI-assisted setup.
enrichment.seo_title: Wix Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Website Builder Guide
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Compare Wix pricing plans, features and use cases. See how the drag-and-drop website builder and ecommerce platform from Tel Aviv-based Wix.com works in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Wix is a cloud-based website building and business-management platform founded in 2006 in Tel Aviv, Israel by Avishai Abrahami, Nadav Abrahami and Giora Kaplan. Wix.com Ltd. went public on the NASDAQ (ticker WIX) in November 2013 and today operates as a large, publicly traded software company with offices across Israel, the United States, Europe, Latin America and Asia; reported headcount has ranged roughly between 4,000 and 5,500 employees, with a workforce reduction of about 1,000 roles reported in 2026. Wix's core product lets individuals, freelancers, agencies and small-to-midsize businesses build websites, online stores, blogs, booking systems and web applications without writing code, using a drag-and-drop visual editor (the Wix Editor), a more structured grid-based editor (Editor X / Wix Studio), and AI-assisted site generation tools that create a starting design from a short business description. The platform bundles hosting, a domain, SSL, an app marketplace (Wix App Market), SEO tools, email marketing, and Wix Payments/third-party payment processing under one roof, positioning it as an all-in-one alternative to assembling a website from separate hosting, CMS, and plugin vendors. In 2025 Wix restructured its long-standing plan lineup (which had included Combo, Unlimited, Business Basic, Business Unlimited and Business VIP) into four simplified paid tiers for 2026: Light, Core, Business and Business Elite, priced at $17, $29, $39 and $159 per month respectively on annual billing (higher on month-to-month billing). A free plan remains available but carries Wix branding and ads and lacks ecommerce functionality; the Core plan is the minimum tier required to sell products and accept payments. Beyond the general site builder, Wix operates specialized products including Wix Stores (ecommerce), Wix Bookings (appointment scheduling), Wix Restaurants, Wix Answers (customer support), Velo (a full code development environment for advanced custom logic), and Wix Studio, an editor aimed at agencies and freelance designers building client sites at scale. Wix competes primarily with Squarespace, Shopify (for ecommerce-first use cases), and WordPress-based site builders, and differentiates itself through ease of use, breadth of built-in business tools, and a large template and app ecosystem, while facing recurring criticism around migration lock-in (sites cannot be exported off the Wix platform) and higher costs at scale compared to self-hosted alternatives.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Wix
paragraphs: Wix is an all-in-one website building and business platform that combines hosting, design tools, ecommerce, marketing and customer-management features into a single cloud-based product. It is aimed at solo entrepreneurs, freelancers, agencies and small-to-midsize businesses that want to launch a professional site without hiring a developer.

Founded in 2006 in Tel Aviv by Avishai Abrahami, Nadav Abrahami and Giora Kaplan, Wix has grown into a publicly traded company on the NASDAQ (WIX) with offices across multiple continents. Its product line has expanded well beyond the original drag-and-drop editor to include dedicated tools for online stores, bookings, restaurants, and enterprise-grade custom development through Velo.
Wix's core differentiator is breadth: rather than stitching together separate hosting, CMS, ecommerce and email-marketing vendors, users get all of it bundled into one subscription and one dashboard.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The Wix Editor provides pixel-precise drag-and-drop page building, while Wix Studio offers a more structured, agency-oriented workflow with reusable design assets and client-handoff tools. Wix's AI site generator can produce a starting layout, copy, and images from a short text description of the business.

Wix Stores adds product catalogs, cart and checkout, inventory management and integration with Wix Payments and third-party processors, while Wix Bookings handles appointment scheduling, staff calendars and service payments for service-based businesses.

The Wix App Market extends functionality with hundreds of third-party and first-party integrations, and Velo provides a full JavaScript-based development environment for building custom logic, databases and API integrations on top of a Wix site.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Wix offers a free plan with Wix branding, ads, and no ecommerce capability, aimed at trying the editor before committing. In 2025 Wix consolidated its paid tiers into four simplified plans for 2026: Light at $17/month, Core at $29/month, Business at $39/month, and Business Elite at $159/month, all priced on annual billing (monthly billing is somewhat higher for each tier).

Selling products online requires at least the Core plan, since the free and Light tiers exclude checkout and payment processing. Business and Business Elite add higher-volume ecommerce features, advanced automations, and priority support aimed at growing stores.
Additional costs can include premium apps from the Wix App Market, a custom domain if not already owned, and Wix Payments transaction fees on ecommerce sales.

features.title: Drag-and-drop editor
features.description: Design pixel-precise pages visually without writing code, using the classic Wix Editor or the agency-oriented Wix Studio.

features.title: AI site generation
features.description: Generate a starting website design, layout and copy from a short description of your business.

features.title: Wix Stores ecommerce
features.description: Sell products online with product catalogs, cart, checkout, inventory and payment processing built in.

features.title: Wix Bookings
features.description: Manage appointments, staff calendars and service payments for service-based businesses.

features.title: App Market integrations
features.description: Extend site functionality with hundreds of first-party and third-party apps covering marketing, forms, chat and more.

features.title: Velo custom development
features.description: Build custom logic, databases and API-driven functionality on top of a Wix site using a full code environment.

features.title: Built-in SEO tools
features.description: Manage meta tags, structured data, sitemaps and mobile optimization from a dedicated SEO dashboard.

features.title: Hosting and security included
features.description: Every paid plan bundles managed hosting, SSL, and infrastructure so there is no separate hosting provider to manage.

pros: All-in-one platform bundles hosting, design, ecommerce and marketing in one subscription
Beginner-friendly drag-and-drop editor with a large template library
AI-assisted site generation speeds up initial setup
Broad app marketplace and Velo development environment support advanced customization
Publicly traded, well-resourced company with continuous product investment
cons: Sites cannot be exported or migrated off the Wix platform to another host
Ecommerce features require at least the Core paid plan, unlike some competitors' free tiers
Costs can climb quickly once premium apps and higher-volume ecommerce tiers are added
Design flexibility on the classic Editor can be more rigid than fully custom-coded sites at scale

faqs.question: How much does Wix cost
faqs.answer: Wix's four paid plans for 2026 are Light at $17/month, Core at $29/month, Business at $39/month and Business Elite at $159/month on annual billing, plus a limited free plan with Wix branding and no ecommerce.

faqs.question: Can I sell products with the free Wix plan
faqs.answer: No. Ecommerce and payment processing require at least the Core plan; the free and Light tiers do not support product listings or checkout.

faqs.question: Who founded Wix
faqs.answer: Wix was founded in 2006 in Tel Aviv, Israel by Avishai Abrahami, Nadav Abrahami and Giora Kaplan.

faqs.question: Is Wix publicly traded
faqs.answer: Yes, Wix.com Ltd. has traded on the NASDAQ under the ticker WIX since its IPO in November 2013.

faqs.question: Can I move my Wix site to another host
faqs.answer: No, Wix does not support exporting a site to run it on external hosting; the site remains tied to the Wix platform.

faqs.question: What is Wix Studio
faqs.answer: Wix Studio is a more structured, agency-oriented editor built for freelancers and agencies managing multiple client sites, offering reusable design assets and collaboration tools.

faqs.question: Does Wix support custom code
faqs.answer: Yes, through Velo, Wix's built-in development environment, which allows JavaScript-based custom logic, databases and API integrations.

faqs.question: What is Wix best for
faqs.answer: Wix is best suited for small businesses, freelancers, and agencies that want an all-in-one, code-free way to launch a professional website, store or booking system quickly.

use_cases.title: Small business website and store
use_cases.description: Launch a professional site with an online store, bookings and payments without hiring a developer.

use_cases.title: Agency client site production
use_cases.description: Design and manage multiple client websites efficiently using Wix Studio's reusable assets and collaboration tools.
use_cases.audience: Design agencies and freelance web designers

use_cases.title: Service-business scheduling
use_cases.description: Run appointment booking, staff calendars and service payments for salons, consultants and other service providers.

enrichment.short_description: Wiz is a cloud-native application protection platform (CNAPP) that scans AWS, Azure, Google Cloud, Oracle Cloud and Kubernetes environments to find and prioritize security risks across the full cloud stack.
enrichment.company_size: Large (part of Google Cloud since March 2025/2026, previously 1,000+ employees as an independent company)
enrichment.headquarters: New York City, New York, United States
enrichment.seo_title: Wiz Review: Cloud Security (CNAPP) Platform Now Part of Google Cloud
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Learn what Wiz (wiz.io) does, its CNAPP platform for AWS, Azure, GCP and Kubernetes security, its founding story, and its acquisition by Google Cloud.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Wiz is an Israeli-American cloud security company, headquartered in New York City, that builds a cloud-native application protection platform (CNAPP) used to secure workloads running on Amazon Web Services, Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud Platform, Oracle Cloud Infrastructure, and Kubernetes. The company was founded in January 2020 by Assaf Rappaport, Yinon Costica, Roy Reznik and Ami Luttwak, a team that had previously founded and sold the cloud security company Adallom to Microsoft. Wiz's core technical approach is agentless: rather than requiring software agents installed on every workload, it connects to a customer's cloud accounts via API and builds a full graph of cloud infrastructure, correlating misconfigurations, vulnerabilities, exposed secrets, identity and access risks, and network exposure into prioritized attack-path findings, so security teams can see which combinations of individually low-severity issues actually create a critical, exploitable risk. This unified approach to what had previously been separate tool categories -- cloud security posture management (CSPM), cloud workload protection (CWPP), Kubernetes security posture management (KSPM), data security posture management (DSPM), and cloud infrastructure entitlement management (CIEM) -- is generally credited with popularizing the CNAPP category and driving Wiz's rapid enterprise adoption. Wiz raised capital at a fast pace: a Series B of $250 million at a $1.7 billion valuation in 2021, a Series C of $250 million at a $6 billion valuation later in 2021, a Series D of $300 million at a $10 billion valuation in February 2023, and a Series E of $1 billion at a $12 billion valuation in May 2024, for roughly $1.9 billion in total funding from investors including Insight Partners, Index Ventures, Sequoia, Greenoaks and Lightspeed Venture Partners. On March 18, 2025, Google announced an all-cash agreement to acquire Wiz for approximately 32 billion dollars, a deal described at the time as the largest cybersecurity acquisition ever and the most expensive acquisition in Google's history; the acquisition closed and Wiz became part of Google Cloud in 2026, continuing to operate its platform across multi-cloud environments including AWS and Azure rather than being restricted to Google Cloud only. Wiz is sold to enterprises on a custom, negotiated pricing basis rather than published self-serve tiers, typically scoped to the size of a customer's cloud footprint (workloads, cloud accounts, and modules such as CSPM, CWPP, DSPM, CIEM and container/Kubernetes security). Wiz is important to distinguish from the unrelated website builder Wix (wix.com) and the unrelated LinkedIn prospecting tool Wiza (wiza.co), which share similar names but serve entirely different markets.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Wiz
paragraphs: Wiz is a cloud-native application protection platform built to give security teams a unified, prioritized view of risk across multi-cloud and Kubernetes environments. It connects agentlessly to a customer's cloud accounts and constructs a graph of the entire environment, correlating vulnerabilities, misconfigurations, identity risk, exposed secrets and network exposure to surface the attack paths that actually matter.

The company was founded in January 2020 by Assaf Rappaport, Yinon Costica, Roy Reznik and Ami Luttwak, the same team that previously built and sold cloud security company Adallom to Microsoft. Wiz is headquartered in New York City and grew into one of the fastest-scaling cybersecurity companies of the 2020s.

In March 2025 Google announced it would acquire Wiz for approximately 32 billion dollars in an all-cash deal, the largest cybersecurity acquisition on record; the deal closed and Wiz joined Google Cloud in 2026 while continuing to support other major clouds.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Wiz's platform combines cloud security posture management (CSPM), cloud workload protection (CWPP), Kubernetes security posture management (KSPM), data security posture management (DSPM) and cloud infrastructure entitlement management (CIEM) into a single product, replacing what previously required multiple point tools.

Its signature capability is attack-path analysis: mapping how a combination of individually minor issues, such as an exposed secret plus an overly permissive role plus a public-facing workload, could chain together into a critical breach path, then ranking findings by real exploitability rather than raw vulnerability counts.

Because Wiz connects via cloud provider APIs rather than requiring agents on every host, it can achieve full environment coverage quickly during onboarding, which has been a major driver of its enterprise adoption speed.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Wiz does not publish self-serve pricing; it is sold to enterprises through a custom, sales-led process, with cost generally scoped to the number of cloud workloads, accounts, and which modules (CSPM, CWPP, DSPM, CIEM, container/Kubernetes security) a customer licenses.

Prospective customers typically engage Wiz's sales team for a scoped demo and quote rather than signing up through a published pricing page, which is standard practice for platform-scale enterprise cloud security tools.

features.title: Agentless cloud scanning
features.description: Connects via cloud provider APIs to build a full inventory and risk graph without deploying agents on every workload.

features.title: Attack-path analysis
features.description: Correlates misconfigurations, vulnerabilities, identity risk and exposure to surface exploitable attack chains, not just isolated findings.

features.title: Multi-cloud coverage
features.description: Supports AWS, Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud Platform, Oracle Cloud Infrastructure and Kubernetes environments.

features.title: CSPM and CWPP in one platform
features.description: Combines cloud security posture management and workload protection so teams do not need separate point tools.

features.title: Data security posture management
features.description: Identifies where sensitive data lives across cloud storage and databases and flags exposure risk.

features.title: Cloud identity and entitlement management
features.description: Analyzes IAM roles and permissions to find excessive or risky entitlements across cloud accounts.

features.title: Kubernetes security posture management
features.description: Assesses container and Kubernetes configurations for misconfigurations and runtime risk.

features.title: Risk prioritization dashboard
features.description: Ranks findings by real-world exploitability so security teams can focus remediation on what matters most.

pros: Unifies multiple previously separate cloud security tool categories into one platform
Agentless deployment enables fast, broad environment coverage
Attack-path analysis reduces alert fatigue by prioritizing real, chained risk
Backed by Google Cloud's scale and resources following the 2025-2026 acquisition
cons: Enterprise, custom pricing with no published self-serve tiers makes cost comparison difficult
Primarily built for larger organizations with meaningful multi-cloud footprints, less suited to very small teams
Now owned by Google Cloud, which may raise vendor-neutrality questions for customers on competing clouds
Full platform value typically requires licensing multiple modules, which can add complexity to procurement

faqs.question: What does Wiz do
faqs.answer: Wiz is a cloud-native application protection platform that scans cloud and Kubernetes environments to find, correlate and prioritize security risks such as misconfigurations, vulnerabilities, exposed secrets and identity risk.

faqs.question: Who founded Wiz
faqs.answer: Wiz was founded in January 2020 by Assaf Rappaport, Yinon Costica, Roy Reznik and Ami Luttwak, who had previously founded and sold the cloud security company Adallom to Microsoft.

faqs.question: Is Wiz the same as Wix
faqs.answer: No. Wiz (wiz.io) is a cloud security company for enterprises; Wix (wix.com) is an unrelated consumer and small-business website builder. The similar names are coincidental.

faqs.question: Did Google acquire Wiz
faqs.answer: Yes, Google announced an all-cash agreement to acquire Wiz for approximately 32 billion dollars in March 2025, and the deal closed with Wiz joining Google Cloud, making it the largest cybersecurity acquisition on record.

faqs.question: Does Wiz only work with Google Cloud
faqs.answer: No, Wiz continues to support multiple cloud providers including AWS, Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud and Oracle Cloud Infrastructure even after becoming part of Google Cloud.

faqs.question: How is Wiz priced
faqs.answer: Wiz uses custom, sales-led enterprise pricing rather than published self-serve plans, typically scoped to cloud workload volume and which security modules are licensed.

faqs.question: What is a CNAPP
faqs.answer: A cloud-native application protection platform (CNAPP) is a unified security tool category, popularized in large part by Wiz, that combines posture management, workload protection, identity risk and data security into one platform.

faqs.question: Who are Wiz's main competitors
faqs.answer: Wiz competes with cloud security vendors such as Palo Alto Networks (Prisma Cloud), Orca Security, CrowdStrike, Microsoft Defender for Cloud, and Aqua Security.

use_cases.title: Multi-cloud risk visibility
use_cases.description: Give security teams a single, prioritized view of risk across AWS, Azure, Google Cloud and Oracle Cloud accounts.
use_cases.audience: Enterprise cloud security and DevSecOps teams

use_cases.title: Kubernetes and container security
use_cases.description: Identify misconfigurations and runtime risk in containerized workloads before they reach production.
use_cases.audience: Platform engineering and Kubernetes operations teams

use_cases.title: Compliance and audit readiness
use_cases.description: Continuously assess cloud configurations against compliance frameworks to prepare for audits.

features: Scoped to cloud workload and account volume
Modular licensing across CSPM, CWPP, DSPM, CIEM and Kubernetes security

enrichment.short_description: Wiza is a B2B prospecting tool that turns LinkedIn and LinkedIn Sales Navigator searches into verified email addresses and phone numbers for sales, recruiting and marketing outreach.
enrichment.seo_title: Wiza Review 2026: LinkedIn Email Finder Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how Wiza (wiza.co) turns LinkedIn Sales Navigator searches into verified emails and phone numbers, plus its 2026 pricing plans and key features.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Wiza is a B2B prospecting and contact-data tool, founded in 2019, that specializes in converting LinkedIn and LinkedIn Sales Navigator searches into exportable, verified lead lists. Its core workflow lets a user run a search inside LinkedIn Sales Navigator (or use Wiza's own search interface), then export the matching profiles through Wiza, which looks up and verifies each contact's business email address and, on higher plans, mobile phone number before the list is downloaded. Wiza markets access to a database described as covering more than 850 million contacts and states it is used by more than 500,000 go-to-market professionals, positioning itself primarily against other LinkedIn-to-email tools and general B2B contact databases. Because emails are verified in real time before export, Wiza aims to reduce the bounce rates that plague lists built from stale, pre-scraped databases. The product is delivered as a web app and as a Chrome extension that integrates directly with the LinkedIn and Sales Navigator interface, and it offers native integrations with common CRMs and sales-engagement tools so exported lists can flow directly into a seller's existing pipeline. Wiza's pricing is credit- and seat-based. A free tier provides a small number of verified emails and phone numbers to try the product. The Starter plan is priced at 49 dollars per month per user and includes a base allotment of verified emails and phone numbers with overage pricing for additional lookups, team analytics and CRM integrations. Two higher self-serve tiers are priced at 83 dollars per month (or 990 dollars billed annually) for unlimited email reveals plus per-phone-number pricing, and 166 dollars per month (or 1,990 dollars billed annually) for unlimited email and phone reveals, both including a monthly export cap, AI-assisted research features, and CRM integrations. A custom-priced Team plan, requiring a minimum of three users and billed annually, adds unlimited email and phone reveals for the whole team, advanced fields, CSV import, API access, and a dedicated customer success manager. Pay-as-you-go, non-subscription pricing is also available at roughly 35 cents per email lookup and 1.50 dollars per phone number lookup for occasional users who do not want a recurring plan. Wiza should not be confused with Wix, the website-building platform, or Wiz, the cloud security company, despite the similarity in name -- all three are unrelated companies serving entirely different markets. Wiza's headquarters location is reported as Toronto, Canada in some company-data sources, with the company operating as a lean, mostly remote team of roughly 30 to 40 employees as of recent reporting.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Wiza
paragraphs: Wiza is a prospecting tool purpose-built around LinkedIn and LinkedIn Sales Navigator. Instead of building a general contact database from scratch, it lets sales, recruiting and marketing users run a search directly inside LinkedIn or Sales Navigator, then export the matching profiles through Wiza, which looks up and verifies a business email address (and, on higher plans, a mobile number) for each contact before the list downloads.

Founded in 2019, Wiza has grown into a widely used LinkedIn-to-email tool, claiming access to a database of more than 850 million contacts and use by over 500,000 go-to-market professionals across sales, recruiting and marketing teams.

The product ships as both a web application and a Chrome browser extension that overlays directly on LinkedIn and Sales Navigator search results, so users can trigger an export without leaving LinkedIn's own interface.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Wiza's central feature is real-time email verification: rather than pulling from a static, potentially stale database, it verifies deliverability at the moment of export, which is intended to reduce bounce rates in outbound campaigns.
Beyond email, higher-tier plans add mobile phone number lookups, AI-assisted research on prospects, and advanced contact fields such as job history and company firmographic data.

Native CRM and sales-engagement integrations, API access on the Team plan, and CSV import/export let exported lists flow directly into a seller's or recruiter's existing pipeline without manual re-entry.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Wiza offers a free plan with a small allotment of verified emails and phone numbers, a Starter plan at 49 dollars per month per user, and two higher self-serve tiers at 83 dollars per month for unlimited email reveals and 166 dollars per month for unlimited email and phone reveals, each also available at a discount on annual billing.
A custom-priced Team plan requires a minimum of three users, billed annually, and adds unlimited reveals for the whole team, API access, CSV import, advanced fields, and a dedicated success manager.
For occasional use without a subscription, Wiza also offers pay-as-you-go pricing at roughly 35 cents per verified email and 1.50 dollars per verified phone number.

features.title: LinkedIn Sales Navigator export
features.description: Export search results directly from LinkedIn Sales Navigator into a verified contact list.

features.title: Real-time email verification
features.description: Verifies each business email address for deliverability at the moment of export to reduce bounce rates.

features.title: Mobile phone number lookup
features.description: Reveals verified mobile numbers for contacts on higher-tier plans.

features.title: Chrome extension
features.description: Runs directly inside the LinkedIn and Sales Navigator interface for one-click list building.

features.title: CRM integrations

Real-time email verification helps keep bounce rates low

Large claimed contact database and CRM integrations support end-to-end outbound workflows
cons: Reliant on LinkedIn's platform and search limits, which can affect volume and consistency
Costs scale quickly for teams needing unlimited reveals across multiple seats
Team plan requires a minimum of three users and annual billing, limiting flexibility for small teams

faqs.question: What does Wiza do
faqs.answer: Wiza turns LinkedIn and LinkedIn Sales Navigator search results into verified email addresses and phone numbers that can be exported for sales, recruiting or marketing outreach.

faqs.question: How much does Wiza cost
faqs.answer: Wiza has a free plan, a Starter plan at 49 dollars per month per user, an Email plan at 83 dollars per month, an Email plus Phone plan at 166 dollars per month, and a custom-priced Team plan requiring three or more users.

faqs.question: Does Wiza verify emails in real time
faqs.answer: Yes, Wiza verifies each email address for deliverability at the moment it is exported rather than relying solely on a static pre-built database.

faqs.question: Is Wiza the same company as Wix or Wiz
faqs.answer: No. Wiza is an unrelated LinkedIn prospecting tool; Wix is a website builder and Wiz is a cloud security company. The similar names are coincidental.

faqs.question: When was Wiza founded
faqs.answer: Wiza was founded in 2019.

faqs.question: Can I use Wiza without a subscription
faqs.answer: Yes, Wiza offers pay-as-you-go pricing at roughly 35 cents per verified email and 1.50 dollars per verified phone number for users who do not want a recurring plan.

faqs.question: Does Wiza integrate with CRMs
faqs.answer: Yes, Wiza offers native CRM and sales-engagement integrations so exported contacts can flow directly into an existing sales pipeline.

enrichment.short_description: WonderCMS is a free, open-source flat-file content management system built as just five files, requiring no database or complex configuration to run a simple website.
enrichment.company_size: Small (open-source project maintained by a small community)
enrichment.seo_title: WonderCMS Review: Free Flat-File CMS (5 Files, No Database)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how WonderCMS, a free open-source flat-file CMS, lets you run a website from just five files with no database, plus its features, pricing and use cases.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: WonderCMS is a free, open-source, flat-file content management system designed to be as small and simple as possible: the entire application consists of five files, including a JSON-based text file that serves as the database, meaning it needs no MySQL or other relational database server to operate. The project has existed since 2008 and is licensed under the permissive MIT license, which allows commercial use, modification and redistribution with attribution. Installation is described as a one-step process -- a user unzips and uploads the five files to any PHP-capable web host, and the site is ready to configure through a browser-based admin interface, with no separate database setup, connection strings, or server-side configuration required. Because there is no database layer to query, default WonderCMS installations are reported to score 100 out of 100 on Google PageSpeed tests on both desktop and mobile, making it attractive for users who prioritize raw page-load speed over the plugin-heavy feature depth of larger CMS platforms like WordPress. WonderCMS ships with a theme and plugin installer built into the admin panel, one-click backups and one-click updates, clean and customizable URLs, a built-in file manager, a customizable login URL for security, a configurable homepage, and responsive design out of the box. SEO support is built in as well, letting site owners set a custom title, meta keywords and meta description on a per-page basis without installing a separate SEO plugin. On privacy and security, WonderCMS is positioned as a no-tracking, no-ads, no powered-by-link product that is GDPR compliant by default, and includes full HTTPS support, hashed admin passwords, CSRF protection, single-cookie sessions, and subresource integrity (SRI) checks for its CSS and JavaScript assets. WonderCMS's target audience is developers, freelancers and small businesses who want a minimal-footprint website -- a landing page, a small brochure site, a portfolio, or a simple blog -- without the overhead, attack surface, and maintenance burden of a full database-backed CMS. Its tradeoff versus WordPress or other mainstream CMS platforms is a much smaller plugin and theme ecosystem and fewer built-in features for complex content structures, ecommerce, or multi-author workflows, in exchange for radical simplicity, speed, and a minimal codebase that is easy to audit and self-host.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is WonderCMS
paragraphs: WonderCMS is a flat-file content management system built around extreme simplicity: the whole application is five files, and it stores content in a JSON text file rather than a relational database, so there is nothing to install or configure beyond uploading files to PHP hosting.
The project has been developed since 2008 and is released as free, open-source software under the MIT license, which permits commercial use, modification and redistribution with attribution.

It is aimed at developers, freelancers and small businesses who want to launch a simple site -- a landing page, brochure site, portfolio or small blog -- without the database dependency, plugin sprawl, or maintenance overhead of larger CMS platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Installation is a one-step process: unzip and upload five files, then configure the site through a browser-based admin panel. There is no database server to set up and no connection string to manage.

The admin panel includes a theme and plugin installer, one-click backups, one-click updates, a built-in file manager, clean and customizable URLs, a configurable homepage, and a customizable admin login URL for added security.

Because there is no database query overhead, default installations commonly score 100 out of 100 on PageSpeed tests for both desktop and mobile, and the platform includes built-in per-page SEO fields for title, meta keywords and meta description.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: WonderCMS is completely free and open source under the MIT license; there is no paid tier, subscription, or licensing fee for the software itself.
The only cost to run a WonderCMS site is standard PHP-capable web hosting, which can be very inexpensive given the platform's minimal file size and lack of database requirements.

features.title: Five-file architecture
features.description: The entire CMS is contained in just five files, with no database server required.

features.title: JSON flat-file database
features.description: Stores content in a simple JSON text file instead of MySQL or another relational database.

features.description: Browse and install themes and plugins directly from the admin dashboard.

features.title: Per-page SEO fields
features.description: Set a custom title, meta keywords and meta description for each page without a separate SEO plugin.

features.title: Security hardening
features.description: Includes HTTPS support, hashed admin passwords, CSRF protection, and a customizable admin login URL.

features.title: Privacy by default
features.description: Ships with no ads, no tracking scripts, and no powered-by links, and is GDPR compliant out of the box.

features.title: High page-speed scores
features.description: Default installs commonly score 100 out of 100 on PageSpeed tests due to the lack of database overhead.

pros: Completely free and open source under the permissive MIT license
Extremely lightweight with no database server to install or maintain
Fast default page-load performance thanks to the flat-file architecture
Simple, minimal codebase that is easy to audit and self-host
Privacy-respecting by default with no ads or tracking
cons: Much smaller plugin and theme ecosystem than WordPress or other mainstream CMS platforms
Not designed for complex content structures, ecommerce, or high-traffic multi-author sites
Flat-file storage can become harder to manage at very large content volumes compared to a database
Smaller community means fewer tutorials and third-party resources than larger CMS platforms

faqs.question: Is WonderCMS free
faqs.answer: Yes, WonderCMS is free and open source under the MIT license, with no paid tier or subscription fee for the software.

faqs.question: Does WonderCMS need a database
faqs.answer: No, WonderCMS stores its content in a JSON flat file instead of a relational database like MySQL, so no database server is required.

faqs.question: How do I install WonderCMS
faqs.answer: Installation is a one-step process: unzip and upload the five WonderCMS files to any PHP-capable web host, then configure the site through the browser-based admin panel.

faqs.question: Is WonderCMS good for large websites
faqs.answer: WonderCMS is best suited for small, simple sites such as landing pages, portfolios and brochure sites; it is not designed for complex, high-traffic or ecommerce-heavy websites.

faqs.question: Does WonderCMS support plugins and themes
faqs.answer: Yes, WonderCMS includes a built-in theme and plugin installer accessible directly from the admin dashboard.

faqs.question: Is WonderCMS secure
faqs.answer: WonderCMS includes HTTPS support, hashed admin passwords, CSRF protection, single-cookie sessions and a customizable login URL as standard security measures.

faqs.question: How long has WonderCMS existed
faqs.answer: WonderCMS has been developed since 2008 as a free, open-source flat-file CMS project.

enrichment.short_description: WooCommerce is a free, open-source ecommerce plugin for WordPress that turns any WordPress site into an online store with product listings, cart, checkout, payments and shipping tools.
enrichment.company_size: Large (owned by Automattic, thousands of employees company-wide)
enrichment.seo_title: WooCommerce Review 2026: Pricing, Features and How It Works
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Learn how WooCommerce, the free open-source WordPress ecommerce plugin owned by Automattic, works, what it costs, and its key features for 2026 online stores.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: WooCommerce is a free, open-source ecommerce plugin for WordPress that adds product catalogs, shopping cart, checkout, payments, shipping and order management to any WordPress website, turning it into a fully functional online store. It was originally developed by WooThemes, a WordPress theme shop founded in 2008, which hired developers Mike Jolley and James Koster (previously at Jigowatt) to build the plugin as a fork of the earlier Jigoshop project. WooCommerce launched publicly on September 27, 2011, and grew quickly, passing 1 million downloads by mid-2013 and 4 million by 2014. In May 2015, Automattic -- the company behind WordPress.com, Jetpack, Tumblr and Pocket Casts -- acquired WooThemes and the WooCommerce plugin for a reported price of roughly 30 million dollars, and has continued developing and maintaining it since. Because it is a plugin rather than a hosted platform, WooCommerce's core software is entirely free and open source, distributed through the official WordPress plugin repository, with no platform fee or revenue share on sales -- unlike hosted competitors such as Shopify. Store owners instead pay for the components they choose to combine: web hosting (commonly cited in the 25 to 350 dollar per month range depending on traffic and performance needs), a domain name, an SSL certificate, and optional paid extensions that typically range from 29 to 299 dollars per year each for functionality such as advanced shipping rules, subscriptions, bookings, or specialized payment gateways. Core payment processing is handled through WooPayments (Automattic's built-in payment gateway) or more than 100 supported third-party gateways including Stripe, PayPal and Square. The free core plugin supports unlimited products, unlimited orders, and a full REST API, with built-in tools for tax calculation, USPS label printing, and integrations with major carriers like UPS and FedEx. WooCommerce's extension marketplace and large library of WordPress themes give store owners deep customization control, and because it inherits WordPress's content-management strengths, it is particularly well suited to merchants who want to combine rich content marketing (blogs, landing pages) with ecommerce on the same site. Its main tradeoffs versus fully hosted platforms are that store owners are responsible for their own hosting, security updates, and plugin-compatibility management, which requires more technical involvement than a fully managed SaaS store builder, though managed WooCommerce hosting providers (including WooCommerce's own hosting offering and third parties) exist to reduce that burden.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is WooCommerce
paragraphs: WooCommerce is a free, open-source plugin that adds full ecommerce functionality -- product catalogs, cart, checkout, payments, shipping and order management -- to any WordPress website, making it one of the most widely used ecommerce platforms on the web by installation count.

The plugin originated with WooThemes, a WordPress theme shop founded in 2008, and launched publicly on September 27, 2011, as a fork of the earlier Jigoshop plugin. It grew rapidly, reaching 1 million downloads by 2013, and in May 2015 was acquired by Automattic, the company behind WordPress.com and Jetpack, for a reported 30 million dollars.

Because WooCommerce is a plugin rather than a hosted SaaS product, store owners run it on their own WordPress installation and hosting, giving them full control over their store's code, data and infrastructure, in contrast to closed hosted platforms like Shopify.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The free core plugin supports unlimited products and orders, a full REST API for custom integrations, and built-in tools for tax calculation and shipping label printing, including direct USPS label printing and carrier integrations with UPS and FedEx.

Payments can be processed through WooPayments, Automattic's native payment gateway, or through more than 100 supported third-party gateways such as Stripe, PayPal and Square, giving merchants flexibility in how they accept credit cards, digital wallets and bank transfers.

A large marketplace of official and third-party extensions adds functionality such as subscriptions, bookings, memberships, advanced shipping rules and marketing tools, letting merchants build a store that matches their exact needs rather than accepting a fixed feature set.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The WooCommerce plugin itself is free and open source, with no platform fee or revenue share on sales, unlike hosted ecommerce platforms that charge a percentage of transactions.

Store owners instead pay for hosting (commonly 25 to 350 dollars per month depending on traffic and performance needs), a domain name, and any optional paid extensions, which typically cost 29 to 299 dollars per year each.
Payment processing fees apply per transaction through WooPayments or whichever third-party gateway a merchant chooses, similar to standard card-processing fees across the industry.

features.title: Free open-source core plugin

features.title: WooPayments and 100+ gateways

features.title: Shipping and label printing
features.description: Print USPS shipping labels directly from the dashboard and connect with carriers like UPS and FedEx.

features.title: Extension marketplace
features.description: Add subscriptions, bookings, memberships and advanced shipping rules through paid and free extensions.

features.title: Full REST API
features.description: Build custom integrations and headless storefronts on top of WooCommerce's complete API.

features.title: WordPress content integration
features.description: Combine rich blog and landing-page content with ecommerce on the same WordPress site.

features.title: Tax calculation tools
features.description: Automatically calculate applicable sales tax across regions using built-in and extension-based tools.

features.title: Large theme ecosystem
features.description: Choose from thousands of WordPress themes to fully customize a store's design.

pros: Core plugin is completely free with no revenue share on sales
Full ownership of code, data and hosting since it runs on the merchant's own WordPress site
Combines WordPress's content-management strength with full ecommerce functionality
Massive extension and theme ecosystem for deep customization
Backed by Automattic, a large and well-resourced company, since 2015
cons: Requires managing your own WordPress hosting, updates and plugin compatibility
Paid extensions can add up quickly, offsetting the free core plugin's cost advantage
More technical setup and maintenance burden than fully hosted platforms like Shopify
Performance and security depend heavily on hosting quality and plugin choices

faqs.question: Is WooCommerce free
faqs.answer: Yes, the core WooCommerce plugin is free and open source with no platform fee or revenue share; costs come from hosting, domains and optional paid extensions.

faqs.question: Who owns WooCommerce
faqs.answer: WooCommerce is owned by Automattic, the company behind WordPress.com and Jetpack, which acquired the plugin's creator WooThemes in May 2015.

faqs.question: When was WooCommerce launched
faqs.answer: WooCommerce launched publicly on September 27, 2011, developed by WooThemes as a fork of the earlier Jigoshop plugin.

faqs.question: Do I need WordPress to use WooCommerce
faqs.answer: Yes, WooCommerce is a WordPress plugin and requires an existing WordPress installation to run.

faqs.question: How does WooCommerce compare to Shopify
faqs.answer: WooCommerce is a free, self-hosted plugin giving full control over code and hosting with no revenue share, while Shopify is a fully hosted platform with monthly fees and, on some plans, transaction fees, but less setup and maintenance responsibility.

faqs.question: What payment gateways does WooCommerce support
faqs.answer: WooCommerce supports WooPayments, its native gateway, along with more than 100 third-party payment gateways including Stripe, PayPal and Square.

faqs.question: Are WooCommerce extensions required
faqs.answer: No, the free core plugin includes unlimited products, orders and a full API; extensions are optional and add specific functionality like subscriptions or advanced shipping.

faqs.question: Is WooCommerce suitable for large stores
faqs.answer: Yes, WooCommerce can scale to large catalogs and high traffic with proper hosting, caching and performance optimization, and is used by stores of many sizes.

enrichment.short_description: Woodpecker is a cold email and LinkedIn outreach platform that helps sales teams find prospects, run multi-channel outbound campaigns, and manage deliverability and follow-ups in one workflow.
enrichment.company_size: Medium (approximately 90 employees, publicly traded on the Warsaw Stock Exchange)
enrichment.seo_title: Woodpecker Review 2026: Cold Email and LinkedIn Outreach Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how Woodpecker (woodpecker.co) handles cold email and LinkedIn outreach, its 2026 pricing model, key features, and who it is built for.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Woodpecker is a cold email and LinkedIn outreach platform founded in 2015 in Wroclaw, Poland, built for businesses running outbound sales campaigns. The company has grown into a publicly traded business listed on the Warsaw Stock Exchange under the ticker WPR, employs roughly 90 people, and reports more than 15,000 users across over 100 countries. Woodpecker's product combines prospecting, multi-step cold email campaign sequencing, LinkedIn outreach steps, automated follow-ups, and deliverability tooling (such as email warm-up and inbox rotation) into a single outbound workflow, along with integrations to CRMs and other sales tools so replies and pipeline activity sync with a team's existing systems. The platform's positioning emphasizes deliverability and reply quality over sheer send volume, targeting sales teams, agencies and founders who rely on cold email and LinkedIn as core pipeline-generation channels rather than teams doing high-volume spam-style blasting. Woodpecker's pricing uses a 'contacted prospects' model: rather than charging strictly per seat or per email sent, the price scales with how many new prospects a customer adds to campaigns within a billing cycle, with the base cold-email plan starting at roughly 29 dollars per month for a modest number of contacted prospects and increasing as that ceiling rises. Add-on features are priced separately: LinkedIn automation costs an additional 29 dollars per month per connected LinkedIn account, and certain integrations carry an additional 20 dollars charge. New users can try the platform through a 14-day free trial or a cap of 100 free cold emails, whichever they prefer, before committing to a paid plan. Woodpecker differentiates itself from larger sales-engagement suites like Outreach or Salesloft by focusing specifically on the cold email and LinkedIn outbound use case with a lighter, more accessible interface, making it a common choice for smaller sales teams, founder-led sales, and outbound-focused marketing agencies rather than large enterprise revenue operations teams needing a full CRM-adjacent platform. It should not be confused with the unrelated open-source continuous integration platform Woodpecker CI (woodpecker-ci.org), which shares the Woodpecker name but serves an entirely different developer-tooling market.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Woodpecker
paragraphs: Woodpecker is a cold email and LinkedIn outreach platform built for businesses that rely on outbound prospecting to generate sales pipeline. It brings prospecting, multi-step email sequencing, LinkedIn outreach steps, automated follow-ups and deliverability tools together into a single outbound workflow.

Founded in 2015 in Wroclaw, Poland, Woodpecker has grown into a publicly traded company on the Warsaw Stock Exchange (ticker WPR), with roughly 90 employees and more than 15,000 users spanning over 100 countries.

The platform is positioned around deliverability and reply quality rather than raw send volume, aiming to keep cold outreach out of spam folders while helping sales teams and agencies book more qualified meetings.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Woodpecker supports multi-step, multi-channel campaigns that combine cold email sequences with LinkedIn touchpoints, automated follow-ups triggered by prospect behavior, and built-in deliverability safeguards such as email warm-up and sending pattern controls.
Prospecting tools help users build and enrich contact lists before launching a campaign, while native integrations sync replies, opens and campaign activity with common CRMs and sales tools.

A dedicated deliverability dashboard gives visibility into inbox placement, bounce rates and sender reputation so teams can adjust sending behavior before problems affect an entire domain's email reputation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Woodpecker prices its core cold email plan around a 'contacted prospects' model, starting at roughly 29 dollars per month for a base number of prospects contacted per billing cycle and increasing as that ceiling rises.

LinkedIn automation is billed separately at 29 dollars per month per connected LinkedIn account, and certain integrations carry an additional 20 dollar charge, letting customers add channels and tools incrementally rather than paying for a fixed all-in-one bundle.
New users can start with a 14-day free trial or a limit of 100 free cold emails before choosing a paid plan.

faqs.question: What does Woodpecker do
faqs.answer: Woodpecker is a cold email and LinkedIn outreach platform that helps sales teams run multi-step outbound campaigns with prospecting, follow-ups and deliverability tools.

faqs.question: How much does Woodpecker cost
faqs.answer: Woodpecker's base cold email plan starts around 29 dollars per month using a contacted-prospects pricing model, with LinkedIn automation at an additional 29 dollars per month per account and some integrations costing 20 dollars extra.

faqs.question: Is there a free trial for Woodpecker
faqs.answer: Yes, Woodpecker offers a 14-day free trial or a cap of 100 free cold emails for new users.

faqs.question: Where is Woodpecker based
faqs.answer: Woodpecker was founded in 2015 and is headquartered in Wroclaw, Poland.

faqs.question: Is Woodpecker publicly traded
faqs.answer: Yes, Woodpecker is listed on the Warsaw Stock Exchange under the ticker WPR.

faqs.question: Is Woodpecker the same as Woodpecker CI
faqs.answer: No, Woodpecker (woodpecker.co) is a cold email and LinkedIn outreach SaaS tool, while Woodpecker CI (woodpecker-ci.org) is an unrelated open-source continuous integration platform. They share a name but are entirely different products.

faqs.question: Does Woodpecker support LinkedIn outreach
faqs.answer: Yes, Woodpecker lets users add LinkedIn touchpoints as steps within a multi-channel outbound campaign, priced as an add-on per connected LinkedIn account.

faqs.question: What is the contacted-prospects pricing model
faqs.answer: Instead of charging a flat per-seat fee, Woodpecker prices plans based on how many new prospects a customer adds to campaigns during a billing cycle.

enrichment.short_description: Woodpecker CI is a free, open-source continuous integration and delivery engine, community-forked from Drone CI, that runs pipeline steps in Docker containers with a modernized UI and plugin marketplace.
enrichment.seo_title: Woodpecker CI Review: Free Open-Source CI/CD (Drone CI Fork)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what Woodpecker CI (woodpecker-ci.org) offers: a free, community-run, Apache 2.0 CI/CD engine forked from Drone, with Docker-based pipelines and plugins.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Woodpecker CI is a free, open-source continuous integration and continuous delivery (CI/CD) engine that runs pipeline steps inside Docker containers. It began as a community fork of Drone CI, created after Drone's license changed from the permissive Apache 2.0 license to a proprietary license following its 0.8 release; developer laszlocph forked the last Apache-licensed version of Drone in 2019 to keep a genuinely free and open-source path alive for users who did not want to move to Drone's new commercial license (Drone is now maintained by Harness under a Polyform Small Business license). The first release published under the Woodpecker name was version 0.8.104 on September 9, 2019, and the project has since been maintained and grown by a community of contributors under the woodpecker-ci GitHub organization rather than a single commercial vendor. Woodpecker is licensed under Apache 2.0 and is explicitly positioned as software that will remain free and open source, in direct contrast to Drone's shift toward a paid commercial license -- this is Woodpecker CI's central value proposition and differentiator in the CI/CD space. Architecturally, Woodpecker maintains backward compatibility with Drone's existing YAML pipeline syntax, so teams migrating from Drone can typically reuse their existing pipeline configuration files with minimal changes, while Woodpecker has continued to add its own features on top, including a modernized web UI, trusted/privileged pipeline steps for controlled access to sensitive operations, improved conditional step execution, and an expanding plugin marketplace for extending pipeline functionality. Like Drone, Woodpecker executes each pipeline step inside its own Docker container, giving builds isolated, reproducible environments, and it supports integration with common Git hosting platforms such as GitHub, GitLab, Gitea, Bitbucket and others for triggering pipelines on code changes. Because it is self-hosted and free, Woodpecker CI is typically deployed by teams and organizations that want full control over their CI/CD infrastructure without paying per-seat or per-build SaaS fees, positioning it as an open-source alternative to hosted CI/CD products like CircleCI, GitHub Actions or GitLab CI, as well as a philosophically 'stays free forever' alternative to Drone's now-commercial licensing model. It should not be confused with the unrelated cold email and LinkedIn outreach SaaS tool Woodpecker (woodpecker.co), which shares the Woodpecker name coincidentally but serves an entirely different market.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Woodpecker CI
paragraphs: Woodpecker CI is a free, open-source continuous integration and delivery engine that executes pipeline steps inside Docker containers, giving each build step an isolated and reproducible environment.

The project began in 2019 as a community fork of Drone CI, created after Drone's license changed from Apache 2.0 to a proprietary license following its 0.8 release. Developer laszlocph forked the last Apache-licensed Drone codebase to preserve a genuinely free path for users, and the first Woodpecker-branded release, version 0.8.104, shipped on September 9, 2019.

Unlike Drone, which is now maintained commercially by Harness under a Polyform Small Business license, Woodpecker CI is maintained by a community of contributors under the Apache 2.0 license and is explicitly committed to staying free and open source, which is its core differentiator against both Drone and hosted CI/CD SaaS products.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Woodpecker maintains backward compatibility with Drone's existing YAML pipeline syntax, so teams migrating from Drone can typically reuse their pipeline configuration files with little modification.

On top of that compatibility, Woodpecker has added its own capabilities, including a modernized web UI, trusted pipeline steps for controlled access to privileged operations, improved conditional step execution, and a growing plugin marketplace for extending pipeline behavior.

Each pipeline step runs inside its own Docker container, and Woodpecker integrates with common Git hosting platforms such as GitHub, GitLab, Gitea and Bitbucket to trigger pipelines automatically on code changes.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Woodpecker CI is completely free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license; there is no paid tier, subscription, or commercial license required to use it.

Because it is self-hosted, the only cost to running Woodpecker is the compute infrastructure a team chooses to run it on, giving organizations full control over CI/CD spend compared to per-seat or per-build hosted CI/CD SaaS products.

features.title: Docker-based pipeline execution
features.description: Runs each pipeline step inside its own Docker container for isolated, reproducible builds.

features.title: Drone-compatible YAML syntax
features.description: Supports Drone's existing pipeline configuration format for easy migration from Drone CI.

features.title: Modernized web UI
features.description: Provides an updated interface for managing pipelines, builds and logs compared to legacy Drone.

features.title: Trusted pipeline steps
features.description: Allows controlled, privileged access for specific pipeline steps that need elevated permissions.

features.title: Plugin marketplace
features.description: Extend pipeline functionality with community and official plugins.

features.title: Git host integrations
features.description: Connects with GitHub, GitLab, Gitea, Bitbucket and other Git hosting platforms to trigger builds.

features.title: Conditional pipeline execution
features.description: Run or skip pipeline steps based on branch, event type or other build conditions.

features.title: Apache 2.0 open-source license
features.description: Free to use, modify and self-host, with a stated commitment to remain open source permanently.

pros: Completely free and open source under the permissive Apache 2.0 license
Drone-compatible pipeline syntax makes migration from Drone straightforward
Community-driven governance avoids the vendor license changes that motivated the original fork
Docker-based execution gives isolated, reproducible build environments
No per-seat or per-build fees since it is self-hosted
cons: Requires self-hosting and infrastructure management, unlike fully managed CI/CD SaaS products
Smaller contributor base and ecosystem than major hosted platforms like GitHub Actions or CircleCI
Community-run project may have less predictable release cadence than a commercially backed product
Documentation and enterprise support options are more limited than commercial CI/CD vendors

faqs.question: Is Woodpecker CI free
faqs.answer: Yes, Woodpecker CI is completely free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license, with no paid tier required.

faqs.question: Is Woodpecker CI a fork of Drone
faqs.answer: Yes, Woodpecker CI was forked from Drone CI in 2019 after Drone's license changed from Apache 2.0 to a proprietary license, to preserve a genuinely free and open-source CI/CD option.

faqs.question: Can I migrate from Drone to Woodpecker CI
faqs.answer: Yes, Woodpecker maintains backward compatibility with Drone's YAML pipeline syntax, so existing Drone pipeline configuration files typically work with minimal changes.

faqs.question: Does Woodpecker CI use Docker
faqs.answer: Yes, Woodpecker executes each pipeline step inside its own Docker container, giving builds isolated and reproducible environments.

faqs.question: What Git platforms does Woodpecker CI support
faqs.answer: Woodpecker integrates with GitHub, GitLab, Gitea, Bitbucket and other common Git hosting platforms to trigger pipelines on code changes.

faqs.question: Is Woodpecker CI the same as Woodpecker the sales tool
faqs.answer: No, Woodpecker CI (woodpecker-ci.org) is an unrelated open-source CI/CD platform; Woodpecker (woodpecker.co) is a separate cold email and LinkedIn outreach SaaS product. They share a name coincidentally.

faqs.question: Who maintains Woodpecker CI
faqs.answer: Woodpecker CI is maintained by a community of open-source contributors under the woodpecker-ci GitHub organization rather than a single commercial vendor.

faqs.question: When was Woodpecker CI first released
faqs.answer: The first release under the Woodpecker name, version 0.8.104, was published on September 9, 2019.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted CI/CD for open-source projects
use_cases.description: Run build, test and deployment pipelines without relying on a hosted SaaS CI provider.

use_cases.title: Migrating away from commercially licensed Drone
use_cases.description: Replace a Drone CI installation with a fully free, community-governed alternative using compatible pipeline syntax.

use_cases.title: Cost-conscious internal CI/CD infrastructure
use_cases.description: Run continuous integration and delivery pipelines on owned infrastructure to avoid per-seat or per-build SaaS fees.
use_cases.audience: Engineering teams managing their own infrastructure
